title: NIC Bonding Guide
summary: Understand link count, link speed, bonding mode, hash policy, flow count, utilization target, aggregate throughput, per-flow limits, and redundancy expectations.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool evaluates whether a NIC bonding or teaming design matches the expected throughput and resiliency behavior.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning server network ports, virtualization hosts, NAS links, backup networks, storage paths, redundant uplinks, or multi-NIC compute designs.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible NIC Bonding Planner inputs and explain how each value affects throughput and failover behavior.

items.label: Number of Links
items.description: The number of physical network links included in the bond or team.
items.whyItMatters: More links can add aggregate capacity, redundancy, or both depending on bonding mode.
items.commonMistake: Assuming two links automatically doubles every transfer.

items.label: Link Speed (Gbps)
items.description: The speed of each physical network link.
items.whyItMatters: Per-link speed often limits single-flow traffic even when aggregate capacity is higher.
items.commonMistake: Expecting one connection to use every link at once.

items.label: Bonding Mode
items.description: The teaming method used to combine or protect links.
items.whyItMatters: Mode determines failover behavior, load distribution, switch requirements, and compatibility risk.
items.commonMistake: Configuring a server mode that the switch side does not actually support.

items.label: Hash Policy (if LACP/XOR)
items.description: The traffic fields used to distribute flows across links when hash-based bonding is used.
items.whyItMatters: Hash policy affects whether traffic spreads across links or piles onto one path.
items.commonMistake: Assuming LACP balances perfectly without checking traffic diversity.

items.label: Number of Concurrent Flows
items.description: The number of independent traffic flows available for the bond to distribute.
items.whyItMatters: Many bonding modes improve aggregate throughput only when there are multiple flows to distribute.
items.commonMistake: Testing with many flows and then expecting one large production flow to behave the same.

items.label: Utilization Target (%)
items.description: The intended usable percentage of theoretical aggregate link capacity.
items.whyItMatters: Lower targets leave room for overhead, imbalance, failover, and burst behavior.
items.commonMistake: Counting 100% of aggregate link speed as reliable usable capacity.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the bonding design likely aligns with the throughput and redundancy goal. Watch means mode, hash policy, flow count, or utilization should be reviewed. Risk means the design may create false expectations around aggregate speed or failover behavior.
bullets: Aggregate capacity does not guarantee single-flow capacity.
LACP and hashing depend heavily on traffic diversity.
Redundancy and throughput are related but not the same design goal.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: NIC Bonding supports the Compute flow where storage, backup, virtualization, and host traffic may all depend on realistic network-path behavior.
bullets: Use this guide when backup or storage throughput depends on server network links.
Do not treat link aggregation as a cure for every bottleneck.
Use fewer faster links or a different topology when single-flow limits dominate.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
